EV Charging Equity in California

Are public EV chargers distributed fairly across California’s communities? We analyzed 17,186 public charging stations across 1,711 ZIP code areas, comparing access in socially vulnerable neighborhoods, disadvantaged communities, and urban vs. rural regions.

📊 CDC SVI 2022 🌱 CalEnviroScreen 4.0 ⚡ AFDC March 2025 n = 17,186 chargers 1,711 ZCTAs 825 Urban · 886 Rural
Data Sources
AFDC / NREL
EV Station Locations
CDC / ATSDR
Social Vulnerability Index
OEHHA
CalEnviroScreen 4.0
U.S. Census
ZCTA 2020 Boundaries
💡 Key Findings at a Glance
📈
The Overall Trend Is Misleading
At first glance, the data shows a positive correlation between community vulnerability and charger count — as if more-vulnerable areas actually get more chargers. But this is a Simpson’s Paradox: big cities happen to have both high vulnerability scores and lots of chargers, creating a false link. Once we separate urban and rural areas, the real picture emerges.
Overall ρ = +0.23 — driven entirely by urban density, not equity
🏙️
In Cities, Vulnerability Doesn’t Predict Charger Access
Among California’s 825 urban ZIP codes, there is zero correlation between how socially vulnerable a neighborhood is and how many chargers it has. Whether a city ZIP code is wealthy or struggling, it typically has 7–11 chargers. EV stations in cities follow commercial density (malls, offices, parking garages) — not community need.
Urban ρ = -0.001 (p = 0.97) — statistically zero
🌾
Rural Communities Are the Real Charging Deserts
California’s 886 rural ZIP codes tell a very different story. The most-vulnerable rural communities have a median of just 2 chargers per ZIP code, and many have zero. The slight positive correlation here means more-vulnerable rural towns have slightly more chargers — but “slightly more than almost nothing” is still almost nothing.
Urban Median Chargers
7–11 per ZIP
Rural Median Chargers
0–2
Many rural ZIPs have literally zero chargers
📍
Disadvantaged Communities: More Chargers per Person, but…
Only 26% of California’s chargers sit inside officially designated Disadvantaged Community (DAC) boundaries. That sounds bad — but DAC census tracts are tiny, dense urban areas. Per capita, DAC residents actually have 8× more chargers nearby than non-DAC residents. The real equity issue isn’t proximity to chargers — it’s affordability, home-charging access, and EV ownership rates. Most DAC residents rent apartments and can’t plug in at home.
Non-DAC — chargers / 100k people
33.7
DAC — chargers / 100k people
274.2
≈ 8× the non-DAC rate (dense urban tracts)
🚫
The Clearest Equity Signal: ZIP Codes with Zero Chargers
Across all vulnerability levels, many California ZIP codes have no public chargers at all. Surprisingly, the least-vulnerable areas (Q1) have the highest rate of zero-charger ZIPs (50%) — these are affluent but car-dependent rural communities. The most-vulnerable areas (Q4) still have 16% of ZIPs with zero chargers. For any family without home charging, a zero-charger ZIP code means EV ownership is essentially impossible.
Q1 (Least Vulnerable)
49.8%
Q2
28.7%
Q3
27.6%
Q4 (Most Vulnerable)
16.1%

🗺️ Interactive Map
📈 Overall Equity Analysis
🏠️ Deep Dive — EV Chargers Inside vs. Outside DAC Areas

What are Disadvantaged Communities (DACs)?

CalEnviroScreen 4.0 (CES), developed by the California Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment (OEHHA), identifies Disadvantaged Communities as census tracts that score in the top 25% on a composite index combining pollution burden (ozone, PM2.5, pesticides, toxic releases, traffic, cleanup sites, groundwater threats, hazardous waste, impaired water, solid waste) with population characteristics (asthma, low birth weight, cardiovascular disease, poverty, unemployment, housing burden, linguistic isolation, and educational attainment). These are California’s most environmentally and socioeconomically burdened communities.

We spatially joined all 17,186 public EV charger locations to the CES 4.0 DAC polygon boundaries to determine which stations fall inside vs. outside designated DAC areas.

📊 Full DAC vs. Non-DAC Comparison

MetricNon-DACDAC
Total Charger Stations12,766 (74.3%)4,420 (25.7%)
  ↳ Level 2 Stations11,0263,752
  ↳ DCFC Stations1,740668
% Stations w/ DCFC13.6%15.1%
Total Ports (plugs)36,98513,813
Avg Ports / Station2.903.13
Population37,926,2781,611,945
Chargers / 100k Pop33.7274.2
Ports / 100k Pop97.5856.9

📖 Panel-by-Panel Explanation

(A) Raw Station Count: Only 4,420 stations (25.7%) of California’s 17,186 public EV chargers sit inside DAC boundaries, while 12,766 (74.3%) are outside.

(B) Per-Capita Access: DAC areas have 274.2 chargers per 100k residents vs. only 33.7 in non-DAC areas — an ~8× difference driven by compact, dense urban census tracts.

(C) Charger Stations by Type: Each station is classified by its highest charging level (DCFC > L2 > L1). In DAC areas, 668 stations (15.1%) have DC Fast Charging, vs. 1,740 (13.6%) in non-DAC areas.

(D) Charging Quality Metrics:

🔌 Avg Ports per Station (top) — DAC areas average 3.13 ports/station vs. 2.90 in Non-DAC areas — dac charging hubs tend to be slightly larger.

⚡ DCFC Share (bottom) — DAC areas have 27.9% of ports as DCFC vs. 26.3% in Non-DAC areas. DAC residents depend more on fast public charging since many can’t charge at home.

⚠️ The Raw Count Gap Is Misleading — While only 25.7% of chargers are inside DAC boundaries, DAC census tracts are geographically small. The 274.2 chargers per 100k figure suggests DAC populations actually have higher local density of chargers.
✅ The Real Equity Concern — The per-capita advantage in DAC areas is driven by geographic concentration in California’s densest cities. The equity gap is not about proximity but about affordability and usability: DAC households have lower EV ownership rates, fewer home charging options, and greater reliance on public charging.
🔬 Urban vs. Rural Stratified Analysis

All ZCTAs — Unstratified Baseline

The overall Spearman ρ = +0.227 suggests more-vulnerable ZCTAs have more chargers — but this is driven by urban density confounding.

🏙️ Urban ZCTAs (≥ 1,000 people / mi²)

ρ = -0.001 (p = 0.9710) — No correlation.

🌾 Rural ZCTAs (< 1,000 people / mi²)

ρ = +0.264 (p = 0.0000) — Significant positive correlation.

Key Takeaway: The naive ρ = +0.23 is a Simpson’s Paradox artifact. Urban ZCTAs show zero correlation (ρ = -0.001). The real equity gap is in rural areas, where even the most-vulnerable communities have a median of just 2 chargers per ZIP code.

Urban ZCTAs have 7–11 median chargers regardless of vulnerability. Rural ZCTAs range from 0 to just 2.

📚 Methodology & Data Sources

Data: 17,186 public EV charging stations from the Alternative Fuels Data Center (March 2025). Social vulnerability from the CDC SVI (2022) at ZCTA level. DAC designations from CalEnviroScreen 4.0. Census ZCTA 2020 boundaries for spatial joins.

Method: Each EV station was spatially joined to its ZCTA (for SVI scores) and to CES 4.0 DAC polygons. Spearman rank correlation (ρ) measured the monotonic relationship between SVI and charger count. Urban/rural stratification used a threshold of 1,000 people/mi². Station type classified by highest level (DCFC > L2 > L1).

Limitations: ZCTA boundaries approximate ZIP codes. Per-capita rates for DAC tracts use residential population but chargers may serve commuters. Analysis measures station presence, not utilization, pricing, or uptime.

Data: AFDC (NREL) · CDC SVI 2022 · CalEnviroScreen 4.0 (OEHHA) · Census ZCTA 2020 · Analysis by Forrest Pasturel & Ian Yu — CSOL 208 Data Science Capstone
